title: Throwing a Matched Pair of Sauce Bowls

glossaryTerms.definition: A measuring tool with two adjustable arms used at the wheel to check and match the diameter and height of thrown forms.
The process of pressing rotating clay on the wheel into a perfectly balanced, vibration-free mound before opening. Centred clay feels smooth and non-pulsing under the hands.
Carving excess clay from the foot and base of a leather-hard thrown piece on the wheel using loop tools. Trimming creates the foot ring and refines the base profile.
A raised ring of clay trimmed at the base of a thrown piece that lifts the bowl off the surface and provides a clean, finished foot.
A pyrometric cone that deforms at approximately 1220 degrees C. The standard cone for mid-fire stoneware in an electric kiln.
The wheel technique of drawing clay upward and outward from the base using coordinated inside and outside hand pressure, forming the walls of a thrown piece.

content.text: Making a matched pair of anything on the wheel is one of the clearest tests of throwing consistency. Two sauce bowls - small, wide, and low - thrown from identical weights of clay, measured with
calipers
to the same diameter and height, then glazed to match. The bowls are 150 g of stoneware smooth each, fired to
cone 6

(1220 degrees C) in an electric kiln. The exercise teaches you to use measurements rather than eye estimates, and shows clearly where your throwing varies between pieces. A good pair is not identical, but the difference should not be visible to someone using them.

attrs.title: Studio safety
attrs.body: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only.

content.text: Preparation

Weigh both balls of clay to exactly 150 g each before sitting at the wheel. This is the first point of control: identical clay weight is the foundation for everything that follows. Set your target dimensions before throwing: for a sauce bowl, aim for roughly 10-11 cm rim diameter and 4-5 cm height. Set the calipers to your target diameter before you open the first ball. Write the target measurements down - do not rely on memory between the two throws.
Steps
Center the first ball. Wet the clay and hands, set the wheel to medium-high speed, and centre by pressing down and inward from both sides simultaneously. Test
centering
by resting a finger lightly on the clay while spinning: no pulsing or wobble means it is centred.
Open the floor. Press the thumb of your dominant hand into the centre of the spinning clay to create the floor, leaving 6-7 mm of base thickness. Open to within 2 cm of the bat or wheel head.
Pull the walls. Use two or three
pulling
passes to raise and thin the walls. For a sauce bowl the shape is wide and low: keep the outward flare from the floor and avoid pulling straight up. Aim for walls 5-6 mm thick throughout.

Refine the rim and collar. Use the wooden rib on the inside wall to compress and smooth the clay. Flatten the rim slightly with your fingertips. Check the diameter with the calipers across the rim: hold one arm inside the bowl, one outside, and read the measurement. Adjust if needed by gently pressing inward or drawing outward on the rim.

Check the height with calipers or a ruler held vertically against the outside. Note the actual diameter and height of the first bowl. These become the target numbers for the second bowl, not the original estimates - throw to match the actual first bowl, not the plan.

Cut the first bowl off the wheel head with the wire cutter and set aside to firm. Throw the second bowl following the same sequence. Check diameter and height against your notes at step 4 and 5. Adjust more actively on the second bowl - this is the learning pass.
Trim both bowls when leather-hard. Re-centre each bowl upside down on the wheel and trim the
foot ring
using the loop tool. The foot ring diameter on both bowls should be the same - set the calipers to match.
Trimming
is also the moment to remove excess base weight: a 150 g sauce bowl needs a light base, not a thick plinth.

Bisque fire both bowls together to cone 06 (1000 degrees C). After bisque, apply wax resist to the foot rings, then glaze the bowls using the same glaze and application method. Apply the same number of coats to each. Fire to cone 6 (1220 degrees C).
What success looks like

The two bowls sit side by side and read as a pair. The rim diameter difference is no more than 5 mm, and the height difference no more than 3 mm. The glaze colour and surface are a close match. If you hold them in each hand, they feel the same weight. Any variation is visible as a technical note but not as a visible mismatch to someone setting the table.

items.problem: Second bowl is noticeably wider than the first
items.cause: The outward flare was not arrested early enough on the second pull
items.fix: Check with calipers after the second pull, not just at the end. Correct diameter earlier in the throw rather than compensating at the rim.

items.problem: Walls are uneven in thickness - thin at the top, thick at the base
items.cause: Pull passes started too high instead of beginning from the floor
items.fix: Begin each pull from as low as possible - fingers just above the floor - and move upward in one smooth motion.

items.problem: Foot ring on one bowl is off-centre after trimming
items.cause: Bowl was not re-centred properly when placed upside down for trimming
items.fix: Mark the centre point of the base with the needle tool before trimming and use it to align the foot ring position.

items.problem: Glaze pooled thick in the base of one bowl
items.cause: Glaze application was not even between the two bowls
items.fix: Dip or apply by the same method and time for both pieces. Check for drips inside before loading.

items.cause: Base floor left too thick at opening stage
items.fix: Keep base thickness to 6-7 mm maximum. Check with a needle tool before cutting off the wheel - press the needle through the floor and measure on the tool shaft.
